coredata.dc:title: Large-area color controllable remote carbon white-light light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenfei
Yu, Siu Fung
Fei, Linfeng
Jin, Limin
Pan, Shusheng
Lin, Peng
coredata.dc:description: We disperse carbon nanodots (C-dots), which can be achieved by decomposing organic acid in silane coupling agent, in epoxy oligomer to realize large-area C-dots film on any transparent substrate. Due to the similarity in solubility parameters between C-dots and epoxy resin, aggregation of C-dots in epoxy resin is suppressed. It is found that the C-dots films can support high conversion efficiency (>60%) white-light emission under 460nm illumination. In addition, the corresponding correlated color temperature can be varied between 2500 and 10,000K by controlling the thickness of the C-dots film. We also verify that the absolute performance of C-dots film is compatible with that of the commercial available phosphor film.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of C-dots films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the functionalized C-dots. (b) Cross-section photo of the C-dots in epoxy disposed on to the substrate. (c) Schematic diagram of the remote carbon white-light LED. (d) Plots of light intensity distribution of two orthogonal directions from the surface of the substrate with (Xs and Ys) and without (X0 and Y0) coating of C-dots film. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Plots of integrated intensity and absorption coefficient of the C-dots films with different thickness. The excitation wavelength and power of the blue LED array at room temperature is 460nm and 10W respectively. (b) Plots of the integrated white-light emission intensity versus excitation power of the blue LED array for different thickness of C-dots film at room temperature. (c) Spectra of C-dots films with different thickness. The inset shows the corresponding CIE 1931 coordinate diagram. (d) CCT versus pump power of LED array with different thickness of C-dots films. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Plots of mass loss and exothermal of the C-dots films at different operation temperature. (b) PL intensity of C-dots film versus duration under continuous 460nm excitation at room temperature. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photos of substrates with (A) phosphor and (B) C-dots coated films with either the ‘on’ and ‘off’ of the LED array. Comparison of the emission spectra of the phosphor and C-dots films with CCT of (b) ∼4900, (c) ∼4000 and (d) 3300K. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

White-light emission observed from (a) a 20W blue LED spotlight covered with C-dots film and (b) a large-area flexible polyethylene terephthalate substrate coated with C-dots. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Solubility parameters of surface functional groups and host matrices. Moore gravity constant method states that the solubility parameter, δ, can be expressed as [25]: δ =(ρ/M)∑G, where ρ is the density, M is the molecular weight, and G is the Moore gravity constant of each groups in a molecule.

head.ce:title: Large-area color controllable remote carbon white-light light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Research Institute, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Shenzhen, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Anhui Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanostructures, Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Science, Hefei, Anhui, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Applied Physics, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We disperse carbon nanodots (C-dots), which can be achieved by decomposing organic acid in silane coupling agent, in epoxy oligomer to realize large-area C-dots film on any transparent substrate. Due to the similarity in solubility parameters between C-dots and epoxy resin, aggregation of C-dots in epoxy resin is suppressed. It is found that the C-dots films can support high conversion efficiency (>60%) white-light emission under 460nm illumination. In addition, the corresponding correlated color temperature can be varied between 2500 and 10,000K by controlling the thickness of the C-dots film. We also verify that the absolute performance of C-dots film is compatible with that of the commercial available phosphor film.

ce:section.ce:para: Current trends of white-light light-emitting diodes (LEDs) design shifts to the remote phosphor technology, which required the suspension of rare-earth phosphor film over blue LEDs, has been widely adopted by the manufacturers to produce remote phosphor white-light LEDs [1,2]. As various correlated color temperature (CCT) can be obtained from white-light LEDs by using suitable composition of rare-earth materials, the remote phosphor technology standardizes the use of blue LEDs [3]. Furthermore, the application of remote phosphor technology can avoid prolong heating of rare-earth phosphor. As a result, reliable, low-cost and CCT tunable white-light LED architecture can be achieved simultaneously [4]. Nevertheless, the need of phosphor restricts the further development of remote phosphor white-light LEDs with large emission area [5]. This is due to the shortage supply of phosphor arises from the increase in the consumption of fluorescent lamps after the phase out of incandescent light bulbs [6], and the restriction of high pollution mining and refining of rare-earth materials [7].

Recently, extensive investigations had been concentrated on the use of functionalized carbon nanodots (C-dots) [8,9] to replace ‘phosphor’ in remote phosphor white-light LEDs. This is because carbon is one of the most abundance elements on earth, environmental friendly, and the fabrication procedures of free-standing C-dots film can be compatible with the manufacturing of remote phosphor [10]. In fact, direct deposition of C-dots [11], mixture of C-dots with silicone [12], C-dots embedded inside polymer matrix [13,14] and C-dots/agar composite [15] onto the surface of either deep-blue or ultraviolet (UV) LEDs (i.e., conventional ‘phosphor on chip’ design) had demonstrated white-light emission of various CCT. Furthermore, freestanding large-area C-dots films had been fabricated by either embedding C-dots inside polymer matrix [16] or casting of C-dots in water solution onto a large glass sheet [17]. However, there are two aspects of challenges hinder the realization of large-area ‘remote carbon films’. Firstly, physical dispersion of C-dots in solid media may lead to low doping concentration of C-dots (i.e., low solubility in solid media) so that low photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield is unavoidable. In addition, phase separation and aggregation of C-dots (i.e., due to the high viscosity of liquid media) induce fluorescence quenching [8,9]. Secondly, the use of deep-blue and UV LEDs as the excitation sources will increase the total manufacturing cost of white-light LEDs as the most cost effective LED sources are emitted at wavelength various between 450 and 460nm.

Here, we utilize previously developed high-efficiency white-light emission C-dots film (i.e., C-dots disperse inside epoxy) to realize large-area remote carbon white-light LEDs under 460nm excitation [18,19]. With suitable fabrication procedures, C-dots can be homogeneously dispersed in epoxy without aggregation so that uniform white-light emission can be obtained over a large surface area. This is due to the high solubility of C-dots in epoxy. It can also be shown that CCT of the C-dots film can be adjusted from 2500 to 10,000K through the control of its thickness. Furthermore, the absolute performance of the C-dots film is evaluated by comparing its emission characteristics with the commercial available remote phosphors (i.e., Intematix-ChromaLit™ Round XT/XTS [20]). It is noted that although the maximum emission intensity of C-dots film is about 3 times lower than that of the commercial rare-earth phosphors, the performance of C-dots film is not too far from practical applications. Hence, C-dots films can have potential to replace ‘phosphor’ in white-light LEDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of C-dots films
ce:section.ce:para: Functionalized C-dots were fabricated by decomposing organic acid in silane coupling agent, then the as-prepared C-dots were mixed with commercial epoxy oligomer under vigorous stir and subsequently degassed for 10min in vacuum (i.e., 10−4 bar) environment. Finally, the mixture was cured into solid C-dots film under continuous heating at 60°C for 6h. Concentration of C-dots in epoxy matrix can be controlled from 0% to 100% by varying the mass ratio of C-dots to epoxy (i.e., 50% represents both as-prepared C-dots and epoxy have the same mass). Detailed fabrication procedures of C-dots films can be found in our previous publication [18].

Morphology of the C-dots was investigated by using a JOEL JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM). Thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were performed by using a NETZSCH STA 449 Jupiter thermal analyzer. Photoluminescence was characterized by an EDINBURGH FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer. Emission characteristics of the remote carbon white-light LEDs were measured by using a LABSPHERE light measurement system with CDS-600 spectrometer and LMS-100 integration sphere.

C-dots, which have size varies from 2.0 to 3.6nm and lattice spacing of 0.2nm, are uniformly dispersed inside epoxy (see Figs. S1a and b of the supporting information). The proposed C-dots consist of sp2 graphite structure carbon core and surface organosilane functional groups which support effective photoluminescence under 460nm excitation. Fig. 1 a shows a schematic diagram of the silane functionalized C-dots with A-2120 functional groups. It is believed that the CO functional group leads to board bandwidth (from 400 to 700nm) emission under a range of excitation wavelength [21]. In addition, the presence of OCNH functional group enhances effective absorption of blue excitation [22] (see Fig. S2a). For the C-dots film (i.e., C-dots dispersed inside epoxy), the emission intensity and peak wavelength are a function of the concentration of C-dots. Maximum emission intensity under 460nm excitation can be obtained with 50% concentration of C-dots (see Fig. S2b). As the location of emission peak (i.e., ∼550nm) and emission linewidth (i.e., ∼120nm) of 50% concentration of C-dots under 460nm excitation are similar to that of the rare-earth phosphor, they are the suitable replacement of ‘phosphor’ in remote phosphor white-light LEDs. The conversion efficiency of C-dots is found to be larger than 60% under 460nm excitation at room temperature. A remote carbon white-light LED can be constructed by depositing C-dots film onto a polystyrene substrate (with diameter and thickness of 60 and 1.5mm respectively and dull polished on both surfaces) as the ‘remote carbon’. Scattering and absorption coefficient of the polystyrene substrate at 460nm is estimated to be 1cm−1. Fig. 1b shows the cross-section of a 400μm thick C-dots film on the plastic substrate. It is observed that the substrate can physically support the C-dots film to avoid deformation or vibration, and acts as a light diffuser to homogenously radiate white-light emission from the C-dots film. An array of 460nm LEDs (EPISTAR LED chip [23]), which has a maximum electrical power of 20W, is used to excite the C-dots film. In addition, a light concentrator is placed between the blue LED array and C-dots film to concentrate light onto the C-dots film, see Fig. 1c (and Fig. S3). Fig. 1d compares the distribution of light intensity from the surface of the substrate with and without coating of C-dots film. The variation of intensity is measured along two orthogonal directions of the substrates; see also the insets of Fig. 1d. The intensity distribution observes from both substrates are almost identical and the corresponding radiation profile is mainly related to the LED array and light concentrator. This implies that light is homogenously radiated from the surface of the C-dots film.

Homogeneous light emission from the surface of the C-dots film is mainly due to the solubility of C-dots in the host matrix (i.e., uniform dispersion of C-dots without aggregation). In fact, the solubility of C-dots in the host matrix can be estimated by comparing their solubility parameters. It is noted that the aggregation of C-dots in the host can be suppressed if they have similar solubility parameter. For example, if the difference of the solubility parameters between C-dots and host matrix is less than 0.5 (4.1868J/cm3)1/2, their compatibility can be regarded as excellent. However, C-dots can be considered to be insoluble in the host matrix if their difference of the solubility parameters is larger than 2 (4.1868J/cm3)1/2. Solubility parameter of C-dots is mainly determined by its surface functional groups. Table 1 shows the estimated solubility parameters of some common functional groups of C-dots and polymer matrix by using Moore gravitational constant method [24]. It is noted that the solubility parameters of PEG200, HCOOH and CH3OH, which are the most commonly functional groups of C-dots, are ranged between 13 and 15 (4.1868J/cm3)1/2 [25]. For some polymer matrices such as PDMS, epoxy and PMMA, their solubility parameters are varied between 7.0 and 10.3 (4.1868J/cm3)1/2. As the solubility parameter of these functional groups is larger than that of the polymer matrices by a value of 2 (4.1868J/cm3)1/2, these functionalized C-dots are difficult to be dispersed in polymer matrices homogeneously. On the other hand, the solubility parameter of A-2120 functional groups, which has a value of 9.4 (4.1868J/cm3)1/2, is very close to that of the epoxy. This means that our C-dots with A-2120 functional groups can homogeneously disperse in epoxy matrix without any aggregation. Hence, homogeneous light emission from the surface of the C-dots film can be obtained.

Influence of C-dots film thickness on the integrated emission intensity and CCT of the remote carbon LEDs is studied in Fig. 2 . Absorption coefficient of the C-dots film at the excitation wavelength of 460nm is found to be 80cm−1. In addition, the integrated white-light emission intensity decreases linearly with the increase of the thickness of C-dots film. The reduction of white-light emission with the increase of thickness of the C-dots films is due to the increase of re-absorption of white-light emission from the thick C-dots film. Fig. 2a. Fig. 2b plots the integrated white-light emission intensity versus excitation power of the blue LED array for different thickness of C-dots film. It is observed that the output intensity increases linearly with the excitation power of the LED array and no saturation of white-light emission is observed under high power density illumination. Saturation of white-light emission at high excitation power is not observed in Fig. 2b because the pumped power density of the LEDs is relatively low. Hence, this indicates that the C-dots film has potential to be used in high-power white-light LEDs.

The capability to adjust the chromaticity of light is one of the crucial requirements of white-light LEDs. As depicted in Fig. 2c, overall emission spectrum is dependent on the thickness of the C-dots film. For the C-dots film with thickness of 100μm (curve ‘a’ of Fig. 2c), the corresponding absorption at the excitation wavelength is relatively low so that the resultant correlated color temperature (CCT) is at a high value of ∼10,000K and the CIE 1931 coordinate is equal to (0.277, 0.285). On the other hand, for the thickness of C-dots film increases to 400μm (curve ‘c’ of Fig. 2c), the corresponding CCT reduces to ∼4000K and the CIE 1931 coordinate is (0.376, 0.374). This is because the 460nm excitation is strongly absorbed by the thick C-dots film. In fact, CCT of the remote carbon white-light LED can be adjusted from ∼2500 to ∼10,000K by varying the thickness of the C-dots film. Fig. 2d plots the CCT of the C-dots films versus the electrical power of the LED array. It is noted that the CCT of C-dots films remain unchanged with the excitation power of the LED array. We can write the direct relationship between CCT and thickness of the C-dots films as T =14,069*exp(t/228)+1539K where T is CCT and t is thickness of C-dots film. Hence, when a certain CCT is specified, the requirement of thickness of the C-dots film can be estimated from this formula.

Thermal stability of C-dots film is analyzed by using TGA and DSC and the results are shown in Fig. 3 a. The mass of C-dots maintains at 97.8% for temperature increases to 100°C in the TGA analysis. DSC curve shows an endothermic for temperature below 100°C. This indicates that the corresponding loss of mass is mainly caused by the evaporation of water and other volatile molecules attached to the surface of C-dots. This also illustrates that the C-dots is stable under normal operating temperature (<50°C) of the remote carbon LEDs. The mass can be maintained at 96.9% even the temperature reaches 150°C. Hence, this verifies that the C-dots film has superior thermal stability. PL stability versus excitation duration of C-dots film is also examined. The C-dots film is continuously irradiated by a 460nm blue LED array operated at a typical power of 2W [16,17]. Fig. 3b plots the PL of C-dots film versus duration of irradiation. It is noted that the PL integrated intensity maintains relatively stable over 30days of continuously irradiation at room temperature. The slightly increase in the integrated intensity of white-light emission within the first 10days can be attributed to the reduction of the re-absorption of white-light emission from the C-dots film by the influence of photobleaching effect.

The best way to evaluate the absolute performance of C-dots film is to compare with that of the commercial available rare-earth phosphor films. Fig. 4 compares the emission characteristics between the phosphor films (purchased from Intematix, Intematix-ChromaLit™ Round XT/XTS curves A) and C-dots films (curves B) of different CCT at room temperature. All the samples were excited and measured under the same conditions. Furthermore, the thickness of C-dots films has been optimized to match the spectral profile of the remote phosphor films in order to make their CCT comparable. Fig. 4b compares the emission spectra of phosphor and C-dots films with CCT roughly equal to 4900K. It is observed that the emission spectra of both films are close to each other and their corresponding CIE 1931 coordinate are matched. Integrated emission intensity of both films was also measured. It is found that the output intensity of commercial phosphor film is about 3.5 times higher than that of C-dots film. Fig. 4c and d compare the C-dots films with CCT roughly equal to 4000 and 3300K respectively. The results show that emission intensity of remote phosphor film is 3.9 (4.6) times higher than that of the C-dots film emitted at 4000K (3300K). This indicates that the conversion efficiency of C-dots film at longer wavelength is lower than that at shorter wavelength. Although the emission intensity of C-dots film is less than that of commercial rare-earth phosphor film, their output intensities are in the same order of magnitude. Hence, C-dots films have potential to replace rare-earth phosphor in white-light LEDs.

Other advantages of C-dots film are their capability to fabricate in large area and deposit on any flexible substrate. Fig. 5 a shows a 20W blue LED spotlight with its protection glass of emission area 18×16cm2 replaced by a remote C-dots film to generate white-light emission. Bright white light illumination can be obtained from the blue LED spotlight. Fig. 5b demonstrates C-dots film can be deposited on large-area flexible polyethylene terephthalate substrate with thickness of 0.15mm (dull polished one side, and the substrate’s absorption coefficient at 460nm is 3.2cm−1) to generate white-light emission under 460nm excitation. This flexible substrate can be transformed into a tube-shaped remote cover for the blue LED array by rolling it mechanically. In fact, C-dots film on flexible substrate can be fabricated into any shape.

In conclusion, C-dots films are fabricated by uniform dispersion of functionalized C-dots in epoxy resin without aggregation. This is because the solubility parameters of functionalized C-dots and epoxy resin are compatible. White-light emission is observed from the C-dots film under 460nm excitation and the corresponding conversion efficiency is larger than 60%. On the other hand, it is found that the absorption coefficient of C-dots film is ∼80cm−1 and the total integrated emission intensity is linearly proportional to the excitation power. Hence, CCT of the white-light emission can be adjusted from 2500 to 10,000K by controlling the thickness of C-dots films. In addition, the PL emission intensity of C-dots film remains stable over one month under continuous excitation at 460nm. The C-dots films can also be coated on any transparent substrate and the maximum achievable white-light emission area is mainly limited by the size of substrates. Furthermore, although the maximum emission intensity of C-dots film is just one-third to that of the Intematix-ChromaLit™, they are of the same order of magnitude. Hence, this indicates that C-dots film has the potential to replace rare-earth phosphor in white-light LEDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61378071). We acknowledged the engineering support from Dr. Lu Wei of the University Research Facilities in Materials Characterization and Device Fabrication (UMF) – Centre for Electron Microscopy.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2014.12.107.
